question: Which project did I start first, the Ferrari model or the Japanese Zero fighter plane model?

retrieval_results.context:
User is building a 1/48 scale Japanese Zero fighter plane, started about a month ago, plans to add a pilot figure, ground equipment such as fuel drums and ammo crates, and will add texture and weathering to the airfield diorama for a realistic battle‑worn look.
User is building a 1980s-era Ferrari 288 GTO model, starting the project about three weeks ago on a rainy Sunday afternoon, spending roughly five hours assembling the basic chassis and engine block, and has been working on it sporadically since.
User wants to add a racing mechanic figure, miniature toolbox, possibly a racing helmet, pit board, and a miniature trackside scene with grandstands and other racing cars to the Ferrari 288 GTO model, focusing on balance and subtlety to create a compelling story.
User attended a local model building club meetup, met John who showcased a highly detailed WWII-era bomber model, was inspired to add more complexity to his own models, and learned a paint chipping technique using masking tape to create sharp, defined edges for a chipped look.
User intends to focus next on the paint job using the new airbrush compressor to achieve a smooth, even finish, and has researched weathering and aging techniques, planning to add subtle wear and tear to the model for realism.
User applied pre-made Ferrari 288 GTO decals to the engine compartment and body about a week ago, found the application process straightforward but required patience to avoid air bubbles and wrinkles, and is satisfied with the results.
User recently acquired a new airbrush compressor and acrylic paints, has experimented with it on test pieces, is pleased with the smoother, more even paint coats it produces, and plans to use it for the Ferrari model's paint job while seeking tips for achieving smooth, even coats.
User is considering adding additional features like opening doors or movable parts to their models and is asking for tips or recommendations on how to incorporate these details.
User plans to add wiring, hoses, and potentially engine components such as cylinders or pistons to the engine compartment, and also wants to add interior details like textured seats and a more detailed dashboard to increase complexity.
